Missing input validation..title: Input Validation
Missing input validation..explanation: Input validation is the practice of checking all external inputs before processing them. Failing to validate inputs can lead to application crashes, unexpected behavior, and severe security vulnerabilities (such as SQL injection, XSS, or remote code execution). Ensuring data is of the expected type, range, and format makes your application highly robust and secure.

Deeply nested logic..title: Flatten Nesting (Avoid Arrow Anti-Pattern)
Deeply nested logic..explanation: Deeply nested conditional structures (like nested if-statements) make code extremely difficult to read, reason about, and maintain. This is often referred to as the 'Arrow Anti-Pattern' or 'Pyramid of Doom'. By restructuring your code using early returns (guard clauses), you keep the main execution path clean, flat, and readable.

No error handling..title: Robust Error Handling
No error handling..explanation: Applications interact with unpredictable environments (network drops, missing files, malformed API payloads). Without defensive error handling, a single unexpected exception can crash the entire process. Utilizing try-catch blocks or structured error propagation helps you gracefully recover from errors and present clean diagnostic logs or UI messages to the user.

Refactor nested code..title: Refactoring Nested Logic
Refactor nested code..explanation: Code readability drops significantly with each level of nesting. Use inversion of conditional logic, extract inner logic to helper functions, or use standard collection operations (like map, filter, or reduce) to convert complex procedural structures into clear, flat declarative code.

Code looks good!.title: Write Clean, Maintainable Code
Code looks good!.explanation: Even when your code meets the immediate functional requirements, writing clear, readable, and well-documented code is essential for team collaboration and long-term maintenance. Aim to use descriptive naming conventions, keep functions focused (Single Responsibility Principle), and document non-trivial architecture decisions.
